title: Recruiting Freshmen: Why the Best Companies Find You Before You Find Yourself

source.original: The Next Biggest Moat in AI — Jaya Gupta

body_html:
The strongest institutions don't wait for talent to arrive fully formed. They find ambitious people early—often as college freshmen—before their self-concept has hardened, before they know what they want to be famous for, before they can distinguish between the work they're good at and the person they're trying to become.

This isn't predatory. It's architectural. A great company gives emerging talent a language for their own ambition. It says: the thing you've been circling around but haven't known how to name can happen here. You can become the person who moved the Mars timeline, the person who was in the room when the frontier shifted, the person whose work became undeniable. That narrative, delivered at the right developmental moment, is more powerful than any compensation package.

Cash can close candidates, but it rarely converts them. The distinction matters enormously. Closing is transactional—a bidding war resolved by economics. Converting is identity-level: the candidate begins to see themselves as a different kind of person because of where they work. The companies that convert early don't just win a hire; they shape the trajectory of someone's entire career before alternatives even crystallize.

This is why elite firms—from McKinsey to Anthropic—invest disproportionately in campus presence and early pipeline. They understand that optionality, belonging, and the feeling of being destined for something are emotions that operate most powerfully before a person has committed to a fixed professional identity. Reach them at 21 and you're not competing against other offers. You're competing against an unformed sense of self.

The implication for founders is direct: if you're only recruiting people who already know exactly what they want, you're fishing in the shallowest pool. The deepest talent often doesn't yet know what it is—it only knows it wants to matter. Build a shape that gives that hunger a name, and the best people will find you before the market even knows they exist.
